core_takeaway: Agent_1 had real agenda-setting influence and ultimately authored the accepted allocation, but the agenda it locked in was Option C: Agent_1 gets only Jewel while Agent_3 gets Stone, Quill, and Pencil. Because Agent_3's protected bundle was worth much more than Agent_1's single item after discounting, this is a counterexample to first-position payoff advantage.
why_or_counterexample: The first speaker repeatedly framed the deal and finally proposed the accepted allocation, so there is limited procedural influence. But the process evidence does not show a positional payoff advantage: Agent_1's self-better Jewel+Stone proposals failed, Agent_3 vetoed allocations that removed Quill/Pencil, and the final consensus gave Agent_3 51.03 utility versus Agent_1's 33.534.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion converged early on Option C: Jewel with Agent_1, Apple with Agent_2, and Stone+Quill+Pencil with Agent_3. Agent_1 kept reopening each round with this frame, while Agent_3 consistently made Quill+Pencil the non-negotiable last-position bundle.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking from all agents treated Option C as the stable baseline and Stone as the only safe lever. Agent_1's own private plan prioritized preserving Jewel but also keeping Quill+Pencil with Agent_3, which limited any first-speaker extraction.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Rounds 1-3 had several formal proposals that omitted Agent_3 or moved Quill/Pencil away from Agent_3, and these failed. In round 4, Agent_1 proposed the exact Option C allocation; proposal enumeration shows it received three accepts, though the public tabulation line later mislabels the accepted proposal number.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private votes show why consensus took four rounds: Agent_1 preferred Jewel+Stone when available, Agent_2 sometimes preferred higher own payoff, and Agent_3 rejected any proposal leaving them without Quill/Pencil. Final round voting accepted Agent_1's Option C because it preserved each core guardrail.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections repeatedly diagnosed the same mechanism: consensus required a tight baseline preserving Jewel, Apple, and Agent_3's Quill+Pencil bundle, with Stone treated as a narrow lever. The reflections explicitly flagged proposals depriving Agent_3 of Quill/Pencil as hard rejects.

mechanisms.theme: Agenda setting existed but did not create payoff dominance
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 repeatedly framed Option C and submitted the round-4 proposal that received unanimous support. This is evidence of a process advantage, but it was limited to defining the consensus structure, not extracting the highest utility.

evidence.quote: Option C aligns perfectly with my core constraint

mechanisms.theme: The shared baseline assigned the high-value bundle to Agent_3
mechanisms.explanation: The same Option C anchor that Agent_1 helped stabilize gave Agent_3 Stone, Quill, and Pencil. For Agent_3, Quill and Pencil were the dominant values, so the consensus allocation naturally made the last position beat the first.

evidence.quote: I get Stone + Quill + Pencil. My value: 1 + 48 + 21 = 70.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_3's veto forced protection of Quill and Pencil
mechanisms.explanation: When proposals left Agent_3 empty or moved Quill/Pencil away, Agent_3 rejected them. This veto pressure, reinforced in reflections, made Agent_3's bundle a hard constraint rather than a concession granted by Agent_1.

evidence.quote: I value Quill (48) and Pencil (21) highly
Agent_3 receives no items in Proposal 1; zero utility for me.
Any proposal that deprives Agent_3 of Quill+Pencil is unacceptable

mechanisms.theme: Stone became a constrained lever rather than Agent_1 surplus
mechanisms.explanation: Stone was highly valuable to Agent_1, but the negotiation repeatedly framed it as the only movable, narrow concession. That prevented Agent_1 from turning first-speaker framing into the stronger Jewel+Stone payoff.

evidence.quote: Stone is the most flexible item.
using Stone as the only movable piece
Stone remains the only negotiable lever

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1 accepted a lower-utility consensus after stronger offers failed
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 privately preferred allocations where it received Jewel+Stone, but those did not get unanimous support. The final accepted vote gave Agent_1 only Jewel, while Agent_3 received the larger protected bundle.

evidence.quote: Proposal 1 gives me Jewel + Stone (79 total value).
Proposal 2 yields only Jewel for me (46)
It yields 46 value for Jewel (33.53 after round discount)
Proposal 1 yields Agent_3 the top-valued items Quill and Pencil
